title: Ordered Fields & the Gap in ℚ

blocks.content: Last lesson left us with a paradox. The rationals are dense: between any two of them sits another, so as you zoom in you never see a gap. Yet we claimed ℚ is still missing points. How can something be missing from a line that looks completely solid?

Here's where the missing point hides. Draw a right triangle with both legs equal to 1. By the Pythagorean theorem its hypotenuse has length √2. Around 500 BC a follower of Pythagoras discovered that this length is NOT a fraction — a result so unsettling to a school that worshipped whole-number ratios that, legend says, it was kept secret. We are about to rediscover their scandal.

Before we hunt the gap, fix the setting. ℚ comes with +, −, ×, ÷ and an order ≤ that plays nicely with them: you can add the same thing to both sides, multiply by a positive number, and so on. A structure with these arithmetic and order rules is called an *ordered field*. ℝ is one too. The only thing that will distinguish ℝ from ℚ in this entire unit is a single extra property — *completeness* — and that is the star of the show.

blocks.latex: (\mathbb{Q}, +, \cdot, \le) \text{ is an ordered field: } x \le y \implies x + z \le y + z, \quad (x \le y,\ 0 \le z) \implies xz \le yz

steps.prompt: Hunt for the gap yourself. Choose a fraction $p = m/n$ between 1 and 2 and watch $p^2$. The line splits into a region where $p^2 < 2$ and one where $p^2 > 2$. Push $p$ up and down: you can land as close to 2 as you like from either side, but no fraction ever makes $p^2$ exactly 2.
Collect every fraction on the low side into one set: $A = \{\, p \in \mathbb{Q},\ p > 0 : p^2 < 2 \,\}$. Does $A$ have a *largest* element?

choices.label: Yes — some rational in $A$ is the biggest.
No — given any $p$ in $A$, a slightly larger rational is still in $A$.

feedback.correct: Right. Whatever $p$ you pick in $A$, there's a tiny rational $\varepsilon > 0$ you can add so that $(p+\varepsilon)^2$ is still below 2 — so $p+\varepsilon$ is also in $A$, and bigger. No element is ever the largest; $A$ creeps upward forever without a top.

incorrect.message: Try to name that biggest rational $p$. Whatever it is, you can nudge it up by a tiny amount and stay under 2 — so $p$ wasn't actually the biggest. There's always room above.
Test the claim: take any $p$ with $p^2 < 2$. Can you always add a small rational and keep the square below 2? If yes, no element can be the largest.

steps.prompt: Time for the Pythagoreans' scandal. Arrange the steps of the classic proof — by contradiction — that *no* rational squares to 2.

items.label: Since $m^2$ is even, $m$ is even; write $m = 2k$.
Suppose, for contradiction, $p = m/n$ in lowest terms (m, n share no common factor) with $p^2 = 2$.
Substituting gives $4k^2 = 2n^2$, so $n^2 = 2k^2$ — hence $n^2$ is even and $n$ is even.
Then $m$ and $n$ are both even, contradicting 'lowest terms.' So no such rational exists.

feedback.correct: That's the proof exactly. The fraction's own 'lowest terms' assumption is what collapses: m and n turn out to share the factor 2, which is impossible. So √2 simply cannot be written as a ratio of integers.

incorrect.message: Build the chain of consequences. Start by assuming a fraction $m/n$ in lowest terms with square 2; square it to learn $m$ is even; substitute $m = 2k$ to learn $n$ is even; then spot the contradiction with 'lowest terms.'

blocks.content: Put the two discoveries together. ℚ is a perfectly good ordered field — arithmetic and order behave flawlessly — and yet it is *incomplete*. We just proved that √2 is not one of its members.

And the set A showed us exactly *where* the hole is. A has no largest element; it climbs toward a boundary and stops just short. That boundary — the smallest number that is ≥ every element of A — is precisely a *least upper bound*. In ℚ, that boundary point is missing.

This is the whole game. The single property ℚ lacks is: every non-empty set that is bounded above has a least upper bound. Add that property and you get ℝ. The next lessons build the language of bounds and suprema carefully, so we can return and prove that √2 really does live in ℝ.

choices.label: Yes — it has +, −, ×, ÷ and a compatible order.
No, because √2 is missing from it.

feedback.correct: Yes. Being an ordered field is only about arithmetic and order, and ℚ satisfies all of those rules.

incorrect.message: A missing √2 is a *completeness* failure, not a field-or-order failure. ℚ still adds, multiplies, divides, and orders perfectly — so it is an ordered field.
Check the definition: an ordered field needs +, −, ×, ÷ and a compatible order. ℚ has all of these.

items.prompt: The standard proof that √2 is irrational is a proof by ___.

choices.label: direct construction of √2 as a fraction

feedback.correct: Exactly — we assumed √2 = m/n in lowest terms and derived an impossibility.

incorrect.message: Induction proves a statement for all $n$ by a base case and a step. Here we instead *assumed the opposite* and reached an absurdity — that's contradiction.
We can't construct √2 as a fraction — that's the whole point we disproved. Instead we assumed it could be and hit a contradiction.
Recall the structure: assume a fraction in lowest terms squares to 2, then show m and n are both even. That impossibility is the hallmark of proof by contradiction.

items.prompt: In ℚ, the set $A = \{ p > 0 : p^2 < 2 \}$ fails to have a ___.

feedback.correct: Right. A is bounded above (by 2, say) but its least upper bound would be √2, which isn't in ℚ — so no supremum exists *within* ℚ.

incorrect.message: A is full of positive numbers, so it certainly has lower bounds (0, for instance). The trouble is at the *top* of A, not the bottom.
A is far from empty — 1 is in it, since $1^2 < 2$. What it lacks is a least upper bound inside ℚ.
A climbs toward √2 and stops short. The thing missing in ℚ is the boundary it approaches — its least upper bound.
